summary: DeVault advocates for expanding Tor usage beyond just web browsing to cover everyday network traffic like IRC, email, DNS lookups, and system updates. He explains that Tor works at the TCP level and can be used with any application that supports SOCKS proxies. He acknowledges the speed tradeoff but argues that many background tasks don't need high speed. He also recommends running mobile traffic through Tor via Orbot, with whitelists for bandwidth-heavy apps. The core argument is that defaulting to secure should be the norm, and that widespread Tor adoption creates protective noise for those who need it most.
key_points: Tor operates at the TCP level and can secure far more than just web browsing, including IRC, email, DNS, and system updates
The speed penalty of Tor is acceptable for many background tasks where latency doesn't matter
Users should layer SSL/TLS on top of Tor to protect against malicious exit nodes
Encrypting trivial traffic creates noise that helps protect people who genuinely need anonymity
The default posture should be secure unless there's a specific reason not to be
money_quotes: If you don't have a good reason to remain insecure, you should default to secure.
Securing trivial traffic makes more noise to hide the things you care about.
The NSA targets people who use Tor. If you "have nothing to hide", then you can help defend those who do by adding more noise and giving agencies that engage in illegal spying a bigger haystack.
Tor can't help you if you compromise yourself.
ISPs can't inject extra ads and tracking into things you're using over Tor.
key_insight: Defaulting to encrypted traffic via Tor — even for mundane tasks — is both a personal security practice and an act of solidarity that provides cover for those who need anonymity most.
